main_summary.summary: Reverend Dr. Harry Blake, born 1934 in Arkansas and raised on the Ashley plantation in Madison Parish (Tallulah, Louisiana) and later the Wood Spur plantation north of Shreveport, recounts his trajectory from a sharecropper's son to SCLC field staff and a six-decade pastorate at Mount Canaan Baptist Church in Shreveport. Interviewed October 3, 2013 by David Cline of Virginia Tech for the Civil Rights History Project, Blake describes sharecropping debt-peonage, voter-registration suppression under Louisiana's constitution-interpretation test, his summer employment under white storekeeper H.L. Whitlow in Dixie, Louisiana, and his 1958 entry into the ministry. Recruited by Dr. C.O. Simkins of Shreveport (an SCLC board member) and interviewed by Martin Luther King Jr. in Atlanta in February 1960, Blake joined SCLC's staff on March 1, 1960 and was arrested that same day. He testifies in detail to the September 22, 1963 memorial march in Shreveport for the 16th Street Baptist Church bombing victims, when Public Safety Commissioner George D'Artois and Shreveport police beat him near-unconscious inside Little Union Baptist Church. The interview concludes with Blake's account of housing development, the Mount Canaan Summer Boys Academy, and his homeschool program for at-risk Black boys.
key_themes: Sharecropping and plantation economy in Louisiana
Voter registration suppression and constitution-interpretation tests
SCLC field staff work under Martin Luther King Jr.
Black church as the institutional base of Movement organizing
main_summary.historical_significance:
Blake's testimony documents the Shreveport, Louisiana front of the Civil Rights Movement, which the U.S. Civil Rights Commission rated the most difficult Southern city. He offers first-person account of the September 22, 1963 memorial march for the 16th Street Baptist Church bombing victims, including Commissioner George D'Artois's denial of the parade permit and the subsequent police beating of Blake inside Little Union Baptist Church. The interview preserves rare first-person testimony on the Baton Rouge bus boycott led by Rev. T.J. Jemison (which Blake identifies as the model for Montgomery), on Dr. C.O. Simkins's role connecting Louisiana organizing to SCLC's national board, and on the Shreveport mayor's later confession that he had quietly coordinated arrest locations away from sympathetic churches.

Blake's account also documents the multigenerational moral economy of the rural Black South: his illiterate sharecropper father's blessing of his SCLC enlistment ("I'd be disappointed if you did anything different"), and the pedagogy of resistance Blake learned from his father's strategic use of deference. The interview's late sections offer a critical retrospective on integration outcomes, particularly school integration, that Blake argues redistributed Black students into white schools while closing Black schools and leaving curriculum disparities intact.

chapters.title: Plantation Childhood and Schooling
chapters.summary: Blake describes his parents (Mississippi-born father, Arkansas-born mother), his 1934 birth in Arkansas, and the family's early move to Louisiana. He recounts growing up on two plantations as the son of an illiterate sharecropper, attending a one-room church school where a single college-sophomore teacher covered first through seventh grade, and learning to read and write only as he approached his teenage years.
related_events: 1940s-1950s Black agricultural labor in northeast Louisiana

chapters.title: Mules, Salt, and Pastoral Strategy
chapters.summary: Blake recounts plowing mules in high school and deciding to teach mules his language rather than learn theirs. He explains his father's proverb that you can lead a horse to water and, if you salt his diet, he'll find water on his own. Blake credits this pedagogy as the foundation of his pastoral strategy for persuading reluctant congregations, while warning that the same technique enabled Jim Jones and David Koresh to lead followers to mass suicide.

chapters.title: First Pastorates and Mount Canaan
chapters.summary: Blake locates his two plantations (Ashley in Madison Parish near Tallulah; Wood Spur north of Shreveport) and describes his liberation from the plantation through college and marriage. He outlines his ministerial trajectory from rural one-Sunday and two-Sunday churches in 1958 through his 1966 call to Mount Canaan Baptist Church in the Allendale neighborhood of Shreveport, where the building was so dilapidated it later collapsed.

chapters.title: Sharecropper Debt and Race Relations
chapters.summary: Blake describes the plantation economy as a system of perpetual indebtedness in which sharecroppers owed their housing, food, mules, and implements to the plantation commissary and were forbidden to leave without another farmer paying their debt. He explains the social compact in which white plantation owners would protect "their" Black workers from the draft and from jail provided the workers stayed within the racial caste line, comparing the system to contemporary American credit-card debt.
related_events: World War II draft board manipulation

chapters.title: Registering to Vote in Caddo Parish
chapters.summary: Blake describes Louisiana's voter-registration barriers: a registered voter had to vouch for the applicant (nearly impossible when only whites were registered), identification was required, and applicants had to interpret the U.S. Constitution to the registrar's satisfaction and compute their exact age in years, months, and days. Blake circumvented the residency requirement by using a friend's city address and studied the test materials to register at twenty-one.

chapters.title: H.L. Whitlow and Father's Influence
chapters.summary: Blake recounts his summer employment at H.L. Whitlow's general store in Dixie, Louisiana, where Whitlow practiced employment fairness uncommon for a 1950s white Southern storekeeper and was called "a nigga lover" by white peers. Blake describes negotiating his wage from $15 to $25 a week and progressing to cashier and $300/month by high-school graduation. He attributes his stance on dignity under Jim Crow to his father, who taught him that one could call a white man a dog if one knew how, without surrendering manhood.

chapters.title: Joining SCLC and First Arrests
chapters.summary: Blake describes his attentiveness to the Baton Rouge bus boycott led by Rev. T.J. Jemison around 1953-1955, which he identifies as the model for the Montgomery boycott. He recounts reading Stride Toward Freedom and meeting Martin Luther King Jr. as his Bishop College commencement speaker. Dr. C.O. Simkins, a Shreveport SCLC board member, arranged his February 1960 interview with King in Atlanta; Blake joined SCLC's staff March 1, 1960 and was arrested that same day for "careless and reckless driving" while sitting in a parked car. An October 1960 assassination attempt fired a bullet through the windshield of his car.

chapters.title: Wyatt Walker Arrest and SCLC Selection
chapters.summary: Blake describes a Shreveport service where Dr. King was preaching and the Ku Klux Klan protested outside. Blake and SCLC executive director Dr. Wyatt Tee Walker were ordered back inside by the chief; Walker refused, citing freedom of assembly, and the two were jailed for "mental observation," a charge Walker later joked Blake had never been cleared of. Blake notes the U.S. Civil Rights Commission rated Shreveport the most difficult city in the South, citing the plantation environment as a discipline of obedience, and recounts his family's support and his father's blessing of his Movement enlistment.

chapters.title: Little Union Baptist Beating
chapters.summary: Blake details the September 22, 1963 memorial march planned to honor the four girls killed in the 16th Street Baptist Church bombing. Commissioner of Public Safety George D'Artois denied the permit at the last possible moment; Blake redirected the gathering into Little Union Baptist Church. After the service, Commissioner D'Artois and police entered the vestibule, beat Blake inside the church, and dragged him to the sidewalk where police struck his head with nightsticks. Believed dead, he was driven to Dallas for treatment by his family, who feared white-doctor sabotage in Shreveport.

chapters.title: Mount Canaan and Community Development
chapters.summary: Blake describes confronting Commissioner D'Artois and the Shreveport mayor in 1966 to secure cooperation for community development at Mount Canaan. The mayor confessed that he had quietly directed police to arrest Blake's protesters away from sympathetic Black churches because he was "a politician." Blake secured eight acres of city land for $35,000, restricted to housing, recreation, education, or religious use; the church then built 120 units of low-income housing, a daycare center, and the Mount Canaan high-rise senior tower.

chapters.title: Integration Critique and Black Church Power
chapters.summary: Blake argues that the Movement targeted immoral practices (segregated transit, lunch counters, restrooms) but that the integration outcome the South delivered was not what activists had asked for. He maintains the Movement asked for equal funding and capable teachers for Black schools, not necessarily integrated schools, and that the bussing-based integration closed Black schools while leaving curriculum disparities intact. Blake identifies the Black church as the enduring source of Black political power because Sunday gatherings provide a captive audience for community organizing.

chapters.title: Summer Boys Academy and Homeschool
chapters.summary: Blake describes his current ministry focus on Black male education, prompted by statistics that prison-cell counts are projected from fourth-grade Black male performance. He recounts the Mount Canaan Summer Boys Academy, started three summers earlier, where one student advanced from second- to sixth-grade reading level. He describes the case of Walter, a twelve-year-old congregant struggling with fourth-grade material, leading the church to launch a homeschool program with a retired teacher serving five boys eight to three daily.
related_events: Mount Canaan Summer Boys Academy launch
